coredata.dc:title: Facile one-step hydrothermal synthesis toward strongly coupled TiO2/graphene quantum dots photocatalysts for efficient hydrogen evolution
coredata.dc:creator: Min, Shixiong
Hou, Jianhua
Lei, Yonggang
Ma, Xiaohua
Lu, Gongxuan
coredata.dc:description: The coupling of semiconductor photocatalysts with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) has been proven to be an effective strategy to enhance the photocatalytic and photoelectrical conversion performances of the resulted composites; however, the preparation of semiconductor/GQDs composites usually involves several time-inefficient and tedious post-treatment steps. Herein, we present a facile one-step hydrothermal route for the preparation of GQDs coupled TiO2 (TiO2/GQDs) photocatalysts using 1,3,6-trinitropyrene (TNP) as the sole precursor of GQDs. During the hydrothermal process, TNP molecules undergo an intramolecular fusion to form GQDs, which simultaneously decorate on the surface of TiO2 nanoparticles, leading to a strong surface interaction between the two components. The effective coupling of GQDs on TiO2 can effectively extend the light absorption of the TiO2 to visible region and enhance the charge separation efficiency of TiO2/GQDs composites as a result of GQDs acting as a photosensitizer and an excellent electron acceptor. These key advances make the TiO2/GQDs photocatalyst highly active towards the H2 evolution reaction, resulting in 7 and 3 times higher H2 evolution rate and photocurrent response at optimal GQDs content than TiO2 alone, respectively. This study provides a new methodology for the development of high-performance GQDs modified semiconductor photocatalysts for energy conversion applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: TiO2
Graphene quantum dots
Hydrothermal synthesis
Photocatalyst
Hydrogen evolution

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst pubtype ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and characterization of TiO2/GQDs photocatalysts
Photocatalytic hydrogen evolution activity of TiO2/GQDs photocatalysts
Photocatalytic mechanism for enhanced H2 generation over TiO2/GQDs photocatalysts

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram for the preparation of TiO2/GQDs photocatalyst by a one-step hydrothermal reaction. (b–d) TEM and high-magnification TEM (HRTEM) images of GQDs. The inset in planel c shows the HRTEM images of individual GQD. (e–g) TEM and HRTEM images of TiO2/GQDs-15 photocatalyst.

(a) FT-IR spectra of pure TiO2 and TiO2/GQDs-15 photocatalysts. (b) Raman spectra of GQDs, pure TiO2, and TiO2/GQDs-15 composite. (c–f) XPS survey spectrum (c) and High-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s (d), O 1s (e), and Ti 2p (f) core level for TiO2/GQDs-15 photocatalyst.

(a) XRD patterns of pure TiO2 and TiO2/GQDs photocatalysts. (b) UV–vis diffuse reflection spectra of pure TiO2 and TiO2/GQDs photocatalysts. Inset is the optical photos of photocatalysts on FTO substrate.

(a) Time courses for H2 evolution over pure TiO2 and TiO2/GQDs photocatalysts. (b) The rate of H2 evolution over pure TiO2 and TiO2/GQDs photocatalysts. (c) Long-term photocatalytic test of TiO2/GQDs-15 photocatalyst during the repeated runs. Reaction conditions: photocatalyst, 20mg; ethanol aqueous solution, 100mL, 10% (v/v); light source, 250W high-pressure mercury lamp.

(a) Unbiased transient photocurrent response of pure TiO2 and TiO2/GQDs photocatalysts electrodes in 0.5M Na2SO4 at pH 7. (b) Steady-state PL spectra of pure TiO2 and TiO2/GQDs photocatalysts. Excitation wavelength: 280nm. (c) Time-resolved PL emission decay curves of pure TiO2 and TiO2/GQDs photocatalysts. Excitation wavelength: 280nm; detection wavelength: 410nm.
Proposed photocatalytic mechanism for enhanced activity of TiO2/GQDs photocatalyst. (a) photosensitization effect of GQDs and (b) as an electron acceptor and reaction center.

head.ce:title: Facile one-step hydrothermal synthesis toward strongly coupled TiO2/graphene quantum dots photocatalysts for efficient hydrogen evolution

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Beifang University of Nationalities, Yinchuan, 750021, Ningxia Province, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory for Oxo Synthesis and Selective Oxidation, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou, 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory for Oxo Synthesis and Selective Oxidation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The coupling of semiconductor photocatalysts with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) has been proven to be an effective strategy to enhance the photocatalytic and photoelectrical conversion performances of the resulted composites; however, the preparation of semiconductor/GQDs composites usually involves several time-inefficient and tedious post-treatment steps. Herein, we present a facile one-step hydrothermal route for the preparation of GQDs coupled TiO2 (TiO2/GQDs) photocatalysts using 1,3,6-trinitropyrene (TNP) as the sole precursor of GQDs. During the hydrothermal process, TNP molecules undergo an intramolecular fusion to form GQDs, which simultaneously decorate on the surface of TiO2 nanoparticles, leading to a strong surface interaction between the two components. The effective coupling of GQDs on TiO2 can effectively extend the light absorption of the TiO2 to visible region and enhance the charge separation efficiency of TiO2/GQDs composites as a result of GQDs acting as a photosensitizer and an excellent electron acceptor. These key advances make the TiO2/GQDs photocatalyst highly active towards the H2 evolution reaction, resulting in 7 and 3 times higher H2 evolution rate and photocurrent response at optimal GQDs content than TiO2 alone, respectively. This study provides a new methodology for the development of high-performance GQDs modified semiconductor photocatalysts for energy conversion applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen (H2) is a clean and high-density energy carrier proposed to solve the energy shortage and environmental pollution issues [1]. Photocatalytic H2 evolution by using semiconductor photocatalysts and solar energy is a promising strategy for solar-to-fuel conversion [2–10]. Among the investigated photocatalysts so far that have shown sufficient capability for reducing water to H2, metal oxides especially titanium dioxide (TiO2), has shown to be promising one because of its high activity, good photostability, chemical inertness, nontoxicity, and low cost [5]. However, the wide-band gap (E g =3.2eV) and high recombination rate of photogenerated charge carriers of TiO2 greatly limit its practical implementations [7]. Recently, it has been demonstrated that the incorporation of conductive carbon nanomaterials [11], such as fullerene (C60) [12–14], carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [15–17], and graphene [18–25], can greatly improve the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 by providing a rapid electron transfer pathway for enhancing charge separation efficiency and introducing sensitization effect for extending light absorption towards visible light region.

“Molecule-like” graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as a newly emerging carbon nanomaterials of the graphene families [26–28], have received increasing attentions for photocatalysis and photoelectrochemical conversion owing to its combined merits of graphene and quantum dots, such as high electron reservation capability, tunable optical and electronic properties, and plentiful edge defects. Specifically, GQDs itself can be directly used as a metal-free photocatalyst for overall water splitting or to modified semiconductor photocatalysts with enhanced photocatalytic and photoelectrochemical performances [29–44]. For examples, Teng et al. [30] have reported that nitrogen-doped GQDs (NGO-QDs) with both p-type and n-type conductivity domains resulted in an internal Z-scheme charge transfer for water splitting reactions, as a result, the NGO-QDs showed visible light activity for overall water splitting to H2 and O2. Gupta [32] and Chan et al. [33] have shown that GQDs infilled/modified TiO2 nanotube arrays hybrid exhibits improved photocatalytic efficiency for degradation of dyes under UV light irradiation. Pan et al. [35] have observed an enhanced separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs in GQDs by forming heterojunctions with TiO2 and the resulted composites showed a high activity for dye degradation. Guo et al. [36] have reported the utilization of GQDs as a green photosensitizer to functionalize ZnO nanowire arrays and enhanced photoelectrochemical water splitting activity were observed. Hus et al. [37] have observed an enhanced photoelectrochemical H2 production activity on CdSe nanocrystals by loading GQDs, where a type-II vectorial charge transfer mechanism was proposed to responsible for enhanced performances of nanoheterostructures. Moreover, GQDs can be used as an electron transfer mediator in polymer and dye-sensitized photovoltaic devices [38–44]. In above studies for GQDs modified semiconductor materials, it is generally believed that when GQDs decorated on semiconductor surfaces, it can not only act as an electron acceptor but also as a photosensitizer, resulting in an enhanced charge separation efficiency along with an enhanced light utilization efficiency, thus leading to enhanced photocatalytic activity on semiconductor/GQDs photocatalysts [32–37]. However, tedious reactions and post-treatment steps are required for the preparation of those composites. Typically, the GQDs were first prepared and purified and then combined with semiconductors to form semiconductor/GQDs composite photocatalyst [35], where an additional hydrothermal treatment step is typically needed to achieve strong surface integrity between the two components [34–38], which cannot be accessible in one-step synthesis process. Therefore, development of more facile and rapid, as well as low-cost methods for synthesis of semiconductor/GQDs photocatalysts with strong interaction remains highly desirable in terms of their large-scale applications in photo(electro)catalysis.

In the present work, we demonstrate a facile one-step hydrothermal route to synthesis TiO2/GQDs composite photocatalyst, where the 1,3,6-trinitropyrene (TNP) was chosen as the sole precursor of GQDs and transformed to GQDs on the surface of TiO2 nanoparticles via intermolecular fusion of TNP catalyzed by alkaline species under mild hydrothermal conditions. The in-situ formation of GQDs on the surface of TiO2 nanoparticles would ensure a strong chemical interaction between the TiO2 nanoparticles and GQDs. Moreover, the GQDs content in TiO2/GQDs can be easily tuned by simply varying the addition amount of TNP precursor during the preparation processes. By optimizing the amount of TNP, the photocatalytic H2 evolution performance and photocurrent response of the TiO2/GQDs composite photocatalysts could be enhanced by a factor of 7 and 3 compared with that of pure TiO2 nanoparticles under UV light irradiation. In addition, the composite photocatalyst exhibits excellent long-term photocatalytic stability for H2 evolution. Based on the results of UV–vis, photoelectrochemical, and steady-state and time-resolved photoluminescence measurements, a possible mechanism was proposed and the enhanced performances could be attributable to the enhanced charge separation efficiency and utilization efficiency of incident light.

All reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Pyrene was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. TiO2 nanoparticles (P25, 20% rutile and 80% anatase) were purchased from Degussa. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Deionized (DI) water was produced using an YL-100B-D water-purification system (resistivity>15MΩ).

The 1,3,6-trinitropyrene (TNP) was prepared according to the method reported in the literature [35,45]. In a typical synthesis of the TiO2/GQDs photocatalyst, 15mg of TNP was dispersed in 0.2M NaOH aqueous solution (80mL) by ultrasonication (150W, 50kHz) for 4h. Afterwards, 0.5g of TiO2 nanoparticles was added into above suspension and the resulting mixture was sonicated for additional 2h before transferring into a Teflon-lined autoclave (100mL). Next, the hydrothermal reaction was performed in an electric oven at 200°C for 10h. After cooling to room temperature, the product was collected by filtration through a 0.22μm microporous membrane, washed with water and ethanol for several times, and dried in air for overnight. The resulted product was denoted as TiO2/GQDs-15. Following similar preparation procedures for TiO2/GQDs-15, the content of GQDs in TiO2/GQDs photocatalysts was tuned by changing the addition amount of TNP from 5 to 30mg, and the obtained samples were labeled as TiO2/GQDs-5, TiO2/GQDs-10, TiO2/GQDs-20, and TiO2/GQDs-30, respectively. In addition, the exact same synthesis procedure was employed to produce GQDs in the absence of TiO2.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were taken with a Tecnai-G2-F30 field emission transmission electron microscope. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the samples were recorded on a Rigaku B/Max-RB diffractometer with a nickel filtrated Cu Kα radiation operated at 40kV and 40mA. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed on a K-Alphasurface analysis (Thermon Scientific) using X-ray monochromatization. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra were performed on Thermo Nicolet Avatar 380 FT-IR spectrometer. Raman spectra were recorded with a DXR2 Raman microscope (Thermo Scientific) with a 633nm He-Ne laser as excitation source at room temperature. UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu UV-3600 UV–vis-near-IR spectrophotometer equipped with an integrating sphere and BaSO4 powders were used as a reflectance standard. UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained with a TU-1810 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Beijing Persee). Photoluminescence spectra were determined by a Horiba Scientific FluoroMax-4 spectrofluorometer spectrometer. The fluorescence decay times were measured using the Horiba Jobin Yvon Data Station HUB operating in time-correlated single photon counting mode (TCSPC) with the time resolution of 200ps. Nano LED diode emitting pulses at 280nm with 1MHz repetition rate was used as an excitation source. Light-scattering Ludox solution was used to obtain the instrument response function (prompt). The time ranges are 0.055ns/channel in 4096 effective channels.

Photocatalytic hydrogen evolution experiments were performed in a sealed quartz flask (142mL) with a flat window for light irradiation and a silicone rubber septum for sampling. The light source was a 250W high pressure mercury lamp. 20mg of the photocatalyst was dispersed in 80mL of 10% (v/v) ethanol aqueous solution. Prior to light irradiation, the reaction mixture was sonicated for 5min and degassed by bubbling N2 gas for 40min. The amount of H2 evolved was manually analyzed using a precalibrated gas chromatography (Tech comp; GC-7900) equipped with a molecular sieve 5A column and a TCD detector, and N2 was used as the carrier gas.

Photoelectrochemical measurements were performed with an electrochemical analyzer (CHI660E) in a conventional three-electrode configuration. A Pt mesh (1cm2) and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) were used as the counter electrode and the reference electrode, respectively. The supporting electrolyte was 0.5M Na2SO4 solution. A 300W Xe lamp was used as a light source. The photocatalyst working electrodes were prepared by electrophoretic deposition on conducting glass supports (FTO) as following: 40mg of photocatalyst powder and 10mg of iodine were dispersed in 15mL of acetone under stirring and sonicated for 5min. After this process, two FTO electrodes (1.5cm×2.5cm) were inserted parallel with each other in the above homogeneous suspensions with ca. 8mm of distance, and then 10V of bias was applied between them for 3min using the same potentiostat used for photoelectrochemical measurements. After that, the photocatalyst electrodes were dried at 100°C for 12h and store in dark prior to measurements. The unbiased anodic photocurrent was investigated with an amperometric current-time technique.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of TiO2/GQDs photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: It has been reported that TNP molecules can readily fuse to form single-crystalline GQDs with uniform size distribution under mild hydrothermal conditions in the presence of alkaline species such as OH, NH3, or N2H4 as catalysts [35,45]. Inspired by this work, we selected TNP as the sole precursor of GQDs to prepare TiO2/GQDs photocatalysts by using a one-step hydrothermal reaction in NaOH (0.2M) aqueous solutions, as schematically illustrated in Fig. 1 a. Prior to the hydrothermal reaction, the TNP was dispersed into NaOH aqueous solution containing TiO2 nanoparticles with the aid of ultrasonication. During this process, due to the presence of positively charged NO2 groups on TNP molecules [45], the TNP can be readily adsorbed onto the surfaces of negatively charged TiO2 nanoparticles (isoelectric point: 5.8–6.8 depend on particle size [46–48]) at basic conditions via electrostatic interactions, and consequently fused to form GQDs by subsequent hydrothermal treatment, resulting in a strongly coupled TiO2/GQDs photocatalyst. The in-situ formation of GQDs on the surface of TiO2 nanoparticles would ensure a strong chemical interaction between GQDs and TiO2 and endow an improved charge transfer dynamics for photocatalytic H2 evolution reactions. Moreover, the GQDs content in the resulted TiO2/GQDs photocatalysts can be easily tuned by varying the addition amount of TNP precursor, which would simplify the large-scale preparation of GQDs functionalized semiconductor composite photocatalysts for desirable purposes.

Fig. 1b and c shows the typical TEM and HRTEM images of as-prepared GQDs, respectively. The obtained GQDs have a relatively uniform particle size and consist of almost defect-free graphene domains with a clear interplanar spacing of 0.22nm corresponding to graphene (100) planes (see the inset image in Fig. 1c) [35,45]. The average lateral size was statistically computed to be 2.6±0.6nm (Fig. 1d). Fig. 1e shows the low-magnification TEM image of TiO2/GQDs-15 sample. It is found that the GQDs are uniformly distributed throughout the surface of TiO2 nanoparticles. HRTEM (Fig. 1f) shows that the TiO2 nanoparticles are firmly decorated with GQDs to form strongly coupled TiO2/GQDs-15 composite. HRTEM image (Fig. 1g) also reveals the existence of the GQDs in the TiO2/GQDs-15 composite, where a 0.21nm lattice spacing for the graphene (100) planes could also be identified [35].

Fig. 2 a shows the FT-IR spectra of the samples. In contrast to TiO2, several additional vibration peaks arising from the GQDs appear in the spectrum for TiO2/GQDs-15 composite, indicating the presence of GQDs. Besides the CC vibration at 1530 and 1646cm−1, the vibrations for COH/OH at 1358 and 3402cm−1 could also be observed. In addition, a very weak vibration band for CN occurs at 1450cm−1. The existence of GQDs in the TiO2/GQDs-15 composite was further verified by Raman spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. 2b. It can be seen that in the Raman spectrum of pure TiO2 (Fig. 2b), four strong peaks at 637, 514, 391, and 138cm−1 correspond to the Eg(3), A1g +B1g(2), B1g(1), and Eg(1) vibration modes of anatase TiO2, respectively [35]. In the case of the pure GQDs, two obvious peaks at 1581 and 1355cm−1 are observable, which correspond to the G and D bands of GQDs, respectively [35]. In the Raman spectrum of TiO2/GQDs-15 composite, except for the characteristic peaks arising from TiO2, two characteristic peaks of the GQDs can also be identified at slightly higher Raman shifts, further confirming the presence of GQDs and indicating that there is a strong interaction between the two components. The chemical compositions of TiO2/GQDs-15 and the interaction between the two components are studied by XPS analysis. As shown in Fig. 2c, the survey XPS spectrum of TiO2/GQDs-15 sample shows strong signals of Ti 2p, C 1s, and O 1s, as well as negligible signal of N 1s. The Ti 2p and O 1s signals originate from TiO2 and the presence of C 1s and N 1s signals confirm the existence of GQDs in the composite. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum (Fig. 2d) reveals the strong signal of CC at 284.8eV, CO/CN peak at 286.9eV, and the distinguishable CO peak at 287.9eV. The peaks at 529.5 and 531.0eV in the O 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2e) are assigned to the Ti-O and OC/OC bonding, respectively. Moreover, the Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 signals for TiO2/GQDs-15 photocatalyst occur at 458.2 and 464.0eV (Fig. 2f), which are slightly higher than that for pure TiO2, for which the Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 appear at 457.8 and 463.7eV (Fig. S1a, Supporting information). At the same time, the O1s spectrum of TiO2/GQDs-15 shown in Fig. S1b exhibits higher binding energy as compared to TiO2. These results indicate that there is a strong interaction between TiO2 and GQDs in the composite, driven from the probable electron transfer from TiO2 by crossing intimately contacted interface to GQDs [49,50]. It has been extensively demonstrated that GQDs not only has large number of zigzag edges and thereby having the band-gap [11,37], but also contains graphene microdomains that can serve as an electron acceptor [50–52]. Thus, in the TiO2/GQDs composite, the electrons in the TiO2 could transfer to GQDs upon UV light irradiation and participates in following photocatalytic reactions (vide infra) [50].

The XRD patterns of samples in Fig. 3 a reveal that all the TiO2/GQDs composites exhibit the similar XRD patterns with TiO2, no additional diffraction peaks assigned to GQDs could be detectable, which might be due to the small amount and/or high dispersion of GQDs in the composites [35,53]. The light absorption capability of TiO2/GQDs composites was then studied by the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis-DRS). As shown in Fig. 3b, pure TiO2 exhibits no absorption in the visible light region, and only an abrupt absorption onset at 400nm is observable. Compared with white pure TiO2, the color of the TiO2/GQDs samples changes from light yellow to brown (inset in Fig. 3b) with the increasing GQDs contents. Meanwhile, the TiO2/GQDs samples exhibits an enhanced absorption in the visible light region ranging from 400 to 800nm, and the absorption intensity increases gradually with the increase of GQDs contents. More interestingly, it can be clearly seen that there is an obvious absorption peak at ∼500nm in the absorption spectra of TiO2/GQDs composites. The enhancement in background absorption and appearance of the characteristic peak at ∼500nm could be attributed to the presence of GQDs, which exhibits a broad absorption in whole UV–vis region with three characteristic absorption peaks at 232, 371, and 503nm (Fig. S2). Moreover, it is also worthy to be noted that the absorption peak at ∼500nm for TiO2/GQDs composites is more pronounced compared with the GQDs, which could be related to the strong chemical interaction between the TiO2 and GQDs, as evidenced by the above XPS results. These results indicate that the strong coupling of GQDs onto the surface of TiO2 nanoparticles will lead to enhanced light harvesting ability for resulted TiO2/GQDs composites, which may render the formation of more charge carriers upon light irradiation, thus improving their photocatalytic activities of TiO2/GQDs composites [43,50].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic hydrogen evolution activity of TiO2/GQDs photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic H2 evolution activities on TiO2/GQDs photocatalysts from ethanol aqueous solution (10%, v/v) were evaluated under UV light irradiation at room temperature and ambient pressure. Fig. 4 a and b shows the time courses of H2 evolution and the corresponding H2 evolution rates on various photocatalysts, respectively. For comparison purpose, the results obtained on pure TiO2 are also included. Blank experiments under the exact same conditions indicate that no H2 formation could be observed in the absence of any catalysts or light irradiation. The controlled experiment over the GQDs alone also shows no appreciable H2 evolution [30,35]. Pure TiO2 alone can steadily produce H2 but with an extremely low rate (0.3μmolh−1). After coupling with GQDs at a proper content, TiO2/GQDs photocatalysts can effectively enhance the photocatalytic activity of H2 evolution. The rate of H2 production increases with the GQDs content and achieves a maximum on TiO2/GQD-15 sample (2.2μmolh−1), which is higher than that of pure TiO2 by up to 7 times. A further increase in GQDs content rather deteriorates the H2 evolution activity. This can be explained by the fact that although these composites show stronger visible light absorption owing to higher GQDs doping level (Fig. 3b), the formation of GQDs aggregates is inevitable [35], which may not only cause an indirect contact with TiO2 nanoparticles, but also shield the TiO2 nanoparticles from absorbing the incident light, thus leading to a decreased photocatalytic activity, similar to the observations over other carbon-based nanomaterials such as carbon black, graphene, carbon nanotubes, and fullerene coupled TiO2 photocatalysts [12–20].

Fig. 4a also reveals that the rate of H2 evolution over each TiO2/GQDs photocatalyst increases linearly with the irradiation time, indicating that the photocatalytic reaction proceeds steadily over TiO2/GQDs photocatalysts. We also carried out a long-term photocatalytic stability experiment of the TiO2/GQDs-15 photocatalyst. The photocatalytic reaction was allowed to precede for 32h with intermittent evacuation every 8h, as shown in Fig. 4c. The photocatalytic H2 generation activity of TiO2/GQDs-15 remains fairly stable and no obvious deactivation occurs in the repeated runs, revealing the high photocatalytic stability of our photocatalyst.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic mechanism for enhanced H2 generation over TiO2/GQDs photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in above discussions, the incorporation of GQDs is effective to enhance the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 for H2 evolution. On the one hand, it has been previously reported that the photosensitization of semiconductor photocatalysts with GQDs are believed to be mainly responsible for the observed high photocatalytic activity on semiconductor/GQDs photocatalysts [30–37]. Note that our TiO2/GQDs photocatalysts can absorb most of the visible light in the solar spectrum, which will maximum the light utilization efficiency. Given that the reported conduction band (CB) level of GQDs (−3.55 to −3.09eV vs. vacuum energy level) [35,39–41] is less negative than that of TiO2 (−4.2eV vs. vacuum energy level) [35], thus the electrons generated in CB of GQDs upon the light irradiation will easily transfer to the CB of TiO2 due to the large energy offset (0.65–1.11eV) formed at contact interface, which would render an efficient electron-hole separation, and hence enhancing the photocatalytic activity of TiO2/GQDs photocatalysts. On the other hand, it has been reported that the GQDs can also act as an electron collector to enhance the separation of the photogenerated charge carriers [50–52], which is similar to graphene-based materials [54–56]. In order to clarify this role of GQDs, photoelectrochemical and steady-state and time-resolved photoluminescence (PL) measurements were carried out. Fig. 5 a shows the transient photocurrent response for photocatalysts under UV–vis light irradiation at zero bias. It is clearly to see that the pure TiO2 electrode shows a much lower photocurrent, most likely due to the fast recombination of electrons and holes. After coupling with GQDs, the photocurrent response of TiO2/GQDs photocatalyst electrodes is greatly enhanced by 3 times as compared to pure TiO2 at an optimal GQDs content. The observed enhancement in the photocurrent response indicates that the improved charge separation and transportation efficiencies could be obtained for TiO2/GQDs composites due to the presence of GQDs. However, at the higher GQDs loadings, light absorption by GQDs adversely affects the effective excitation of TiO2 nanoparticles, which leads to decreased photocurrent response.

In Fig. 5b, the steady-state PL spectrum of pure TiO2 exhibits an emission band in the wavelength range of 350–550nm, which was assigned to the excitonic band edge emission of TiO2. Compared with pure TiO2, all the TiO2/GQDs samples display a substantially decreased PL emission, and the quenching efficiency of PL emission increases with the increase of GQDs content. This observation reveals that charge recombination of TiO2 was greatly retarded by combination with GQDs. To further understand the charge separation dynamics, the time-resolved PL decay spectra of the pure TiO2 and TiO2/GQDs photocatalysts were recorded at room temperature, as shown in Fig. 5c. It can be seen that the emission decays of the TiO2/GQDs are much faster than that of pure TiO2, suggesting that the incorporation of GQDs provides a rapid electron transfer pathway for enhanced charge separation [37]. These results clearly indicate that the incorporation of GQDs improves, to some extent, the charge separation efficiency of TiO2, and consequently improving the photocatalytic activity of TiO2/GQDs composite materials. However, the quenching efficiency and emission decay of PL of TiO2 by GQDs for TiO2/GQDs samples cannot be completely correlated to their photocatalytic performances, suggesting that the photocatalytic activity of TiO2/GQDs was not simply related to the charge separation factor [57], and equally important, the transfer and the redox ability of charge carrier also play important roles for observed performances. Moreover, it has been demonstrated that GQDs itself can be directly used as a photocatalyst for H2 evolution reaction without the addition of any cocatalysts such as Pt [30], suggesting that GQDs also can serve as a reaction center for H2 evolution reaction. Overall, it can be concluded that the GQDs play synergistic roles in enhancing the photocatalytic activity of TiO2/GQDs photocatalyst, that is, GQDs act as a photosensitizer for maximizing light absorption (Fig. 6 a), an electron acceptor for enhancing charge separation efficiency, and a reaction center for facilitating H2 evolution reaction (Fig. 6b).

In summary, we have developed a facile one-step hydrothermal method for synthesis of strongly coupled TiO2/GQDs photocatalysts by in-situ intramolecular fusion of TNP on the surface of TiO2 nanoparticles. The in-situ formed GQDs firmly decorated on TiO2 nanoparticles due to the chemical interactions between the two components. The incorporation of GQDs has no effect on crystallographic structure of TiO2 (P25) as confirmed by XRD analysis. UV–vis-DRS characterizations revealed that after coupling GQDs onto the surface of TiO2 nanoparticles, the resulted TiO2/GQDs composites exhibited enhanced visible light absorption in the range of 400–800nm. The photoelectrochemical and PL measurements indicated that the GQDs can also act as an effective electron acceptor to facilitate the charge separation of TiO2. Significantly, compared with pure TiO2 nanoparticles, the resulted TiO2/GQDs photocatalysts display enhanced photocatalytic activity for H2 evolution by 7 times and photocurrent response by 3 times. This enhancement could be attributable to the synergistic roles of GQDs that can act as a photosensitizer for improving the light utilization efficiency, but also act as an electron acceptor for reducing the recombination rate of charge carriers. Thus, it is a scalable and cost-effective route for the synthesis of GQDs modified semiconductors as efficient photocatalysts for the photocatalysis and photoelectrochemical conversion.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Science Foundation of China (21463001, 21263001), the Natural Science Foundation of Ningxia Hui Autonomous Region (NZ15102), the Scientific Research Projects of the State Ethnic Affairs Commission (2066204), and the Cultivation Project of National Natural Science Foundation of China (2014QZP04).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2016.11.169.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: K.Q. Lu, Q. Quan, N. Zhang, Y.J. Xu, J. Energy. Chem. 2016, http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jechem.2016.09.015.
